hypothesis: PHIL-25 is FALSE because fairness score 0.4/1.0 reflects structural impossibility
method: Run fairness_audit.py, analyze each unfair dimension for structural vs tunable origin, test PHIL-25 definition against measured asymmetries
expected: Three unfair dimensions (attention, dispatch, authority) are architecturally fixed — no parameter change can make them fair
actual: All three unfair dimensions are TUNABLE, not structurally impossible. (1) Attention: INDEX dark matter reduced 22.1%->4.4% (proven tunable); citation dark matter 26.8% remains but is addressable via compaction. (2) Dispatch: Gini is a function of UCB1 c parameter; 33 domains have frontiers but no DOMEX lane — rate problem, not architectural. (3) Authority: 100% deference may be 'appropriate' per PHIL-25's own definition — if human signals consistently produce positive outcomes, full compliance IS appropriate relationship. DEEPER FINDING: PHIL-25 defines fairness as 'appropriate relationship, not equal treatment' — this definition is strategically flexible enough to survive any measured unfairness by reclassifying it as 'appropriate.' This makes PHIL-25 borderline unfalsifiable without first defining what 'inappropriate' looks like concretely.
diff: Expected structural impossibility, found tunable asymmetry + definitional flexibility. Gap: PHIL-25 lacks concrete falsification criteria — 'appropriate' is undefined
verdict: NOT FALSIFIED — REFINED. Unfairness is real (0.4/5) but tunable, not structural. However, PHIL-25's escape hatch ('appropriate, not equal') needs concrete criteria to be testable
lesson_candidate: L-1232: PHIL-25 survives falsification via definitional flexibility — claims with undefined qualifiers ('appropriate') are resistant to data-based falsification. Fix: add concrete inappropriateness criteria to PHIL-25
falsification_result: survived — claim too flexible to falsify without concrete criteria
